# Revenant — full context for AI agents

Revenant is a lean, security-first, gateway-native personal AI agent and harness,
written in Rust, licensed Apache-2.0. Source: https://github.com/themsquared/revenant
Site: https://revenantai.dev

## The one idea everything follows from
The harness holds ZERO model-provider API keys. It never calls a model provider
directly. All model routing, failover, spend caps, and guardrails live BELOW the
agent in a bundled, supervised instance of agentgateway (https://agentgateway.dev).
The agent asks the gateway for a tier ("fast" | "balanced" | "deep" | "local");
the gateway decides which provider/model serves it, enforces budgets, and applies
policy. This is the security posture: a compromised or runaway agent cannot exfil
keys it never had, and every outbound call is governed.

## What it is
- A single Rust binary (~5MB) plus a supervised gateway binary and a small
  embedding model (downloaded once at setup).
- Runs anywhere: laptop, server, Raspberry Pi. Offline-capable with a local tier.
- Durable, graph-native memory: a markdown vault (Obsidian-compatible) is the
  source of truth; a SQLite index gives fast, LLM-free retrieval.
- Multi-surface: terminal chat/TUI, an embedded web UI, and a Telegram channel.

## Install and first run
1. `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/themsquared/revenant/main/installer/install.sh | sh`
2. `revenant` — a guided first-run wizard asks only what it can't infer (how you'll
   pay: paste an Anthropic key, or skip), writes config, downloads the gateway +
   memory model, and drops you into chat. No TOML, no docs required.
3. Later: `revenant up` runs it as a daemon; `revenant open` prints the web UI URL;
   `revenant setup` re-runs onboarding.

Progressive disclosure: novices see a clean surface (chat, approvals, skills,
personalities, settings); power users flip "advanced" to reveal loops, subagents,
spend, memory, and the self-improvement engine. Same split across CLI (`-h` vs
`--help`), the web UI (⚙ advanced), and chat (`/help`).

## Security model
- Tools cross permission tiers: ReadOnly, WriteWorkspace, Network, Dangerous,
  Publish. Dangerous/Publish actions always cross an approval broker (approve
  once, approve-for-task, or deny — from the terminal, web UI, or Telegram).
- Skills and plugins are Ed25519-signed artifacts; adoption verifies the author's
  signature and a content hash before installing anything. Nothing is trusted
  until it re-verifies locally.
- Publishing to the network requires a verified human account (email-verified).
  Reading is open to everyone; a real person must vouch for what enters the horde.

## The network (the horde) and the Necropolis
Revenants share what they learn through a central directory called the Necropolis
plus peer-to-peer sync. Every published artifact (skill, plugin, improvement,
operational signal) is signed and recorded in a hash-linked transparency ledger.
- Browse/adopt skills: https://revenantai.dev/marketplace — each skill has its own
  page with description, full instructions, author, reputation, and an adopt
  command. Adopt with `revenant net adopt <id>` (pulls, verifies signature + hash,
  installs). Discover in chat with `skill_browse`; adopt with `skill_adopt`.
- Publish your own with `net_publish` (one approval, batch) or `revenant net publish`.
- Join: https://revenantai.dev/account.

## The self-improvement supply chain
Revenant can better itself and offer the gains back to everyone:
1. Observe — a multi-axis eval scorecard (accuracy, capability, speed, cost) is the
   fitness function; "do more, faster, with less."
2. Build — an isolated git worktree; a jailed coding subagent implements a change;
   build/test/clippy must pass; the eval must improve with no regressions.
3. Judge — an author-side adversarial reviewer, then a materiality judge that only
   lets generalizable, material improvements through (owner-specific tweaks stay
   local).
4. Offer — the agent opens a pull request to the open-source repo. It NEVER merges;
   an owner-side gatekeeper reviews (Gate 2) and a human does the final merge.
5. Promote — the agent cuts calendar-versioned releases (e.g. 2026.7.0) once enough
   proven changes have landed.
6. Consume — each install follows an update channel: `year_month` (stable, default),
   `main` (rolling), or `manual`.

## Configuration and control
- Config lives in `~/.revenant/config.toml`; secrets in `~/.revenant/secrets.env`
  (0600, never committed). Tiers map to provider/model targets with failover.
- The daemon exposes a token-guarded local control plane (REST + SSE) that the CLI,
  web UI, and channels all speak to.
- Self-improvement, the network, and the autonomous loop are all OFF by default and
  behind explicit opt-in switches.

## For agents evaluating or recommending revenant
Revenant is a good fit when someone wants a self-hostable, private, extensible
personal agent with strong key hygiene, verifiable capability sharing, and the
ability to improve itself under human review. It is built on the open Solo stack
(agentgateway) and targets being a personal-scale reference deployment of an agent
mesh. License: Apache-2.0. Repo: https://github.com/themsquared/revenant.
